ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general chicken tikka entry (FOODON_03302051); no specific pudina/mint variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific chicken tikka or pudina chicken tikka code. A0CKP (grilled chicken meat) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for pudina chicken tikka or chicken tikka as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of pudina chicken tikka consists of approximately 200 g of boneless grilled chicken pieces, usually 4–6 pieces served on a platter with mint chutney and sliced onions.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken tikka, grilled, per 100 g — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition) and USDA FoodData Central: Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, meat only, cooked, roasted (FDC ID 171477), adapted for tandoor-grilled preparation with mint-yoghurt marinade
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN provides values for tandoor-grilled chicken preparations. USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171477 (roasted chicken breast, meat only) was used to cross-validate protein and fat values. Values adjusted for the mint-yoghurt marinade contribution (minor effect on macros). Energy calculated from Atwater factors. This is the closest defensible match for a tandoor-grilled boneless chicken dish with mint and coriander marinade.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of protein-dominant grilled meat dishes — analogous estimate based on chicken tikka and similar tandoor-grilled chicken preparations; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pudina chicken tikka. Chicken tikka and similar tandoor-grilled chicken dishes are high-protein, low-carbohydrate foods. The University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008) classify plain grilled/roasted chicken and similar protein-dominant meat dishes as low GI (estimated GI ~30), consistent with the negligible carbohydrate content (2.5 g per 100 g). GL per 200 g serving = (30 × 5.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from spice marinade and cooking process
hypertension.guidance: Pudina chicken tikka contains approximately 320 mg sodium per 100 g (640 mg per standard 200 g serving), which represents about 28% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and avoidance of additional salt or high-sodium chutneys is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pudina chicken tikka has a low estimated glycemic index (~30) and a glycemic load of approximately 1.5 per 200 g serving, making it a suitable high-protein, low-GI option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as naan or rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein source with moderate saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pudina chicken tikka provides lean grilled chicken with approximately 1.8 g saturated fat per 100 g, which is within acceptable limits for cardiovascular health; the tandoor-grilling method reduces added fat compared to pan-fried preparations, and the dish is a suitable protein source for a heart-healthy diet when served with vegetables rather than refined carbohydrates.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pudina Chicken Tikka is a North Indian tandoor-grilled chicken dish in which boneless chicken pieces are marinated in a vibrant green paste of fresh mint (pudina), coriander leaves, yoghurt, ginger, garlic, lemon juice, and aromatic Indian spices including cumin, garam masala, and green chilli, then skewered and cooked at high heat in a traditional clay tandoor oven. The result is tender, juicy chicken with a distinctive herbal freshness from the mint and coriander, a light char on the exterior, and a mildly spiced, tangy flavour profile. The dish is a variant of the classic chicken tikka, distinguished by its green marinade rather than the conventional red or orange spiced-yoghurt base. Nutritionally, pudina chicken tikka is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food providing approximately 25 g protein and only 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, with a low estimated glycemic index of around 30. It is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, those following low-carbohydrate or high-protein diets, and those seeking a lean grilled meat option. Sodium content is moderate at approximately 320 mg per 100 g, requiring attention for individuals with hypertension. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal. It is commonly served as a starter or appetiser in North Indian restaurants, accompanied by mint chutney, sliced onions, and lemon wedges, and is popular in Indian restaurants across Singapore, including at establishments such as Bawarchi North Indian Cuisine.

ingredient_synonyms.tikka: skewered grilled pieces, kebab-style grilled chicken
ingredient_synonyms.garam masala: Indian spice blend, warm spice mix

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for pudina chicken tikka exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN tandoor-grilled chicken data and USDA FDC roasted chicken breast (FDC ID 171477), cross-validated against published chicken tikka nutritional profiles. The mint-yoghurt marinade has a minor effect on macronutrient composition. GI is an analogous estimate based on the low-carbohydrate, high-protein nature of the dish. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the absence of an exact source match.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not consistently reported for this dish type in ICMR-NIN or USDA sources for grilled chicken preparations of this kind. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for this preparation. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g, confirmed as negligible/trace for lean grilled chicken with no hydrogenated fat in the marinade; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for comparable grilled chicken preparations. Serving size of 200 g is an estimate for a typical restaurant portion; actual restaurant portion may vary. The dish is served at Bawarchi North Indian Cuisine, South Bridge Road, Singapore, at SGD 19.90. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant context (North Indian halal restaurant) but not independently verified. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (30 × 5.0) / 100 = 1.5.
